step1_rewrite.paragraph: HuNSG-QUAD mice reconstituted with human HSPCs mount NF-κB-dependent, type I interferon, and NLRP3 inflammasome responses after lipopolysaccharide challenge; MCC950 selectively blocks IL-1β and IL-18 without increasing monocyte death, indicating cytokine release can proceed independently of pyroptotic cell death.

sections.title: Abstract
sections.text:
Background

Dysregulated innate immune responses drive the pathogenesis of inflammatory diseases, yet preclinical models often fail to predict human clinical outcomes [CITE: mouse models poor predictors of human inflammatory responses]. NSG-QUAD mice express four human myelopoiesis transgenes to enhance engraftment of human hematopoietic cells, but whether these mice support functional human innate immune responses—particularly inflammasome activation—remains unknown. We reconstituted NSG-QUAD mice with human hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells (HSPCs) and evaluated the development and function of human myeloid cells in vivo.

Methods and Findings

NSG-QUAD mice received human HSPCs and were assessed for myeloid cell engraftment at 6 weeks post-injection. Humanized mice were challenged with lipopolysaccharide (LPS) via intraperitoneal or intranasal routes, and cytokine responses were measured by Luminex. NLRP3 inflammasome inhibition was tested using MCC950. By 6 weeks, NSG-QUAD mice displayed human monocytes, dendritic cells, and granulocytes in peripheral blood, spleen, and liver (Figure 1). Intraperitoneal and intranasal LPS induced NF-κB-dependent cytokines and type I interferon in humanized mice (Figure 2). LPS challenge also triggered NLRP3 inflammasome-mediated secretion of IL-1β and IL-18 (Figure 3). MCC950 specifically abrogated IL-1β and IL-18 production without affecting monocyte viability or NF-κB-dependent cytokine levels (Figure 4).

Conclusions

Humanized NSG-QUAD mice mount functional human innate immune responses, including NF-κB, type I interferon, and NLRP3 inflammasome activation, following LPS challenge. This model provides a platform for studying human innate immunity and testing inflammasome-targeted therapeutics in vivo.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.text:
Dysregulated innate immune responses contribute to the pathogenesis of inflammatory and autoimmune diseases ranging from rheumatoid arthritis to inflammatory bowel disease [CITE: innate immunity in inflammatory disease pathogenesis]. Therapeutic targeting of innate immune mediators, particularly IL-1β blockade, has demonstrated clinical efficacy in autoinflammatory syndromes and select rheumatic conditions [CITE: clinical efficacy of IL-1 blockade in autoinflammatory syndromes]. Despite these advances, the development of innate immune-targeted therapies remains constrained by the limited predictive value of preclinical models.

Mouse models of innate immunity frequently fail to recapitulate human responses due to species-specific differences in immune signaling pathways, receptor expression patterns, and cytokine networks [CITE: species differences in innate immune signaling between mice and humans]. Conventional humanized mouse models, while enabling the study of human adaptive immunity, show limited human myeloid cell engraftment and functionality [CITE: limitations of conventional humanized mouse models for myeloid cell studies]. In vivo platforms that faithfully reproduce human inflammasome responses—critical mediators of IL-1β and IL-18 secretion—are particularly lacking. This gap impedes the preclinical evaluation of inflammasome inhibitors and other innate immune therapeutics in a physiologically relevant context.

NSG-QUAD mice express four human myelopoietic cytokines (M-CSF, IL-3, GM-CSF, and TPO) as transgenes, designed to enhance human myeloid cell development following hematopoietic stem and progenitor cell (HSPC) reconstitution [CITE: NSG-QUAD mouse strain development and transgene expression]. We hypothesized that HSPC-engrafted NSG-QUAD mice would support functional human innate immune responses, including NF-κB activation, type I interferon production, and NLRP3 inflammasome-mediated cytokine secretion. To test this hypothesis, we evaluated human myeloid cell engraftment in peripheral blood, spleen, and liver at 6 weeks post-HSPC injection, then challenged humanized mice with lipopolysaccharide (LPS), a canonical TLR4 agonist and inflammasome priming signal [CITE: LPS as TLR4 agonist and inflammasome priming signal]. We further assessed whether pharmacological inflammasome inhibition with MCC950 would selectively abrogate IL-1β and IL-18 secretion without affecting NF-κB-dependent cytokine production or monocyte viability, thereby validating this platform for testing human-specific innate immune therapeutics.

sections.title: Results
sections.text:
NSG-QUAD mice support rapid human myeloid cell engraftment

NSG-QUAD mice reconstituted with human hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells displayed human myeloid cell populations in peripheral blood, spleen, and liver by 6 weeks post-injection. Flow cytometry analysis revealed human monocytes in all three tissues at this early time-point (Figure 1A). Human dendritic cells were similarly detected in peripheral blood, spleen, and liver at 6 weeks (Figure 1B). Human granulocytes engrafted in the same anatomical compartments within the same timeframe (Figure 1C). [FILL: quantitative engraftment data — percentage of human CD45+ cells, absolute cell counts per tissue, and frequency of each myeloid subset relative to total human leukocytes]

Humanized NSG-QUAD mice mount NF-κB and type I interferon responses to LPS

Intraperitoneal administration of LPS induced NF-κB-dependent cytokine production in humanized NSG-QUAD mice (Figure 2A). [FILL: specific NF-κB-dependent cytokines detected (e.g., TNF-α, IL-6, IL-12p70) and their absolute concentrations or fold-change relative to PBS-treated controls] Intranasal LPS challenge similarly triggered NF-κB-dependent cytokine secretion, demonstrating functional responses to both systemic and mucosal inflammatory stimuli (Figure 2C). Type I interferon was detected in serum following intraperitoneal LPS (Figure 2B) and intranasal LPS (Figure 2D). [FILL: type I interferon subtype measured (IFN-α or IFN-β), quantitative levels in pg/mL or U/mL, and kinetics of peak response]

LPS induces NLRP3 inflammasome activation in humanized NSG-QUAD mice

LPS challenge elicited IL-1β secretion in humanized NSG-QUAD mice (Figure 3A). [FILL: IL-1β levels in pg/mL or fold-change over baseline, time-point of measurement, and route of LPS administration] IL-18 was similarly detected following LPS stimulation (Figure 3B). [FILL: IL-18 levels, kinetics, and comparison to IL-1β response magnitude] Both cytokines are canonical products of NLRP3 inflammasome activation [CITE: IL-1β and IL-18 as NLRP3 inflammasome products], indicating functional assembly of the inflammasome complex in human myeloid cells within the NSG-QUAD microenvironment.

MCC950 selectively inhibits inflammasome-mediated cytokines without affecting monocyte viability

Administration of the NLRP3 inhibitor MCC950 abrogated LPS-induced IL-1β secretion in humanized NSG-QUAD mice (Figure 4A). [FILL: quantitative reduction in IL-1β — percentage inhibition relative to vehicle-treated controls, or absolute concentrations in MCC950-treated vs. vehicle groups, with statistical significance] MCC950 blocked IL-18 production to a comparable extent (Figure 4B). [FILL: IL-18 inhibition data — percentage reduction, absolute levels, and p-value for MCC950 vs. vehicle comparison] Monocyte viability remained unchanged in MCC950-treated mice compared to vehicle-treated controls (Figure 4C). [FILL: monocyte viability percentages in MCC950 vs. vehicle groups, measured by flow cytometry or other method, with n per group and statistical test] NF-κB-dependent cytokines and type I interferon levels were unaffected by MCC950 treatment (Figure 4D), confirming selective inhibition of the NLRP3 inflammasome pathway without broader suppression of innate immune signaling.

sections.title: Discussion
sections.text:
NSG-QUAD mice reconstituted with human hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells developed functional human innate immune cells capable of mounting NF-κB, type I interferon, and NLRP3 inflammasome responses to lipopolysaccharide challenge. The 6-week engraftment timeline observed in this study is substantially shorter than many conventional humanized mouse protocols [CITE: typical engraftment timelines in conventional humanized mouse models], improving experimental throughput for innate immunity research. Detection of human monocytes, dendritic cells, and granulocytes across peripheral blood, spleen, and liver (Figure 1A–C) indicates that NSG-QUAD transgenes support broad myeloid lineage differentiation.

Previous humanized mouse models have shown limited human myeloid function, particularly for inflammasome-dependent responses [CITE: limited inflammasome function in conventional humanized mice]. The NSG-QUAD strain's expression of human M-CSF, IL-3, GM-CSF, and TPO likely enhances myeloid differentiation and survival [CITE: role of human cytokines in myeloid cell development]. Our findings demonstrate that human TLR4 signaling and downstream NF-κB activation remain intact in this model, as evidenced by robust cytokine production following both intraperitoneal and intranasal LPS challenge (Figure 2A, 2C). Type I interferon production (Figure 2B, 2D) indicates functional IRF pathway activation [CITE: TLR4-NF-κB and IRF pathways in human innate immunity], which is critical for antiviral and inflammatory responses in human myeloid cells.

The detection of IL-1β and IL-18 secretion following LPS stimulation (Figure 3A–B) confirms that humanized NSG-QUAD mice support NLRP3 inflammasome activation. MCC950's selective inhibition of IL-1β and IL-18 without affecting monocyte viability (Figure 4A–C) validates the model's utility for testing human-specific therapeutics. The lack of effect on NF-κB-dependent cytokines (Figure 4D) confirms MCC950's specificity for NLRP3 [CITE: MCC950 mechanism of action and specificity for NLRP3], consistent with in vitro human cell data. This pharmacological validation suggests the model can distinguish on-target from off-target drug effects, a critical requirement for preclinical therapeutic development.

This study has several limitations. The model uses a single HSPC source; donor-to-donor variability in engraftment and function requires further characterization. [FILL: whether cord blood or bone marrow HSPCs were used] We assessed responses to LPS, a potent but non-physiological stimulus. Validation with pathogen-associated molecular patterns from specific microbes is needed to establish the model's relevance to infectious disease contexts. Adaptive immune responses were not evaluated in this study; T and B cell function in NSG-QUAD mice remains to be determined. The extent to which tissue-resident human macrophages develop in this model is unclear, as our analysis focused on circulating and splenic myeloid populations.

Humanized NSG-QUAD mice provide a platform for testing innate immune therapeutics, including inflammasome inhibitors, in a human cellular context. The model may enable mechanistic studies of human-specific innate immune pathways that cannot be recapitulated in conventional mouse models. Future applications could include modeling inflammatory diseases driven by dysregulated NF-κB or inflammasome signaling, particularly as clinical development of inflammasome inhibitors advances [CITE: clinical development of inflammasome inhibitors].

Characterizing additional innate immune pathways, such as cGAS-STING and AIM2 inflammasome responses, in this model would expand its utility for studying diverse pattern recognition receptor systems. Testing responses to live pathogens or pathogen-derived stimuli would better reflect physiological immune challenges than synthetic TLR agonists alone. Combining humanized NSG-QUAD mice with disease-relevant genetic variants, such as autoinflammatory mutations in NLRP3 or other inflammasome components, could model patient-specific innate immune dysregulation and facilitate precision medicine approaches to inflammatory disease.
fill_markers: whether cord blood or bone marrow HSPCs were used — needed to specify HSPC source type for reproducibility and interpretation of donor variability

sections.title: Methods
sections.text:
NSG-QUAD mice (NOD.Cg-Prkdcscid Il2rgtm1Wjl Tg(CMV-IL3,CSF2,KITLG)1Eav Tg(TNFSF10)1Dvs/SzJ) were obtained [FILL: vendor and stock number for NSG-QUAD mice] and housed under specific pathogen-free conditions [FILL: institutional animal facility name and accreditation]. All procedures were approved [FILL: IACUC protocol number and institutional approval]. Human hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells were isolated [FILL: HSPC source — cord blood or bone marrow, IRB consent protocol, donor anonymization method]. HSPCs were prepared [FILL: HSPC isolation method and purity assessment] and injected [FILL: injection route — intravenous, intrahepatic, or other] at [FILL: exact cell number per mouse and injection volume]. Engraftment was assessed at 6 weeks post-injection by flow cytometry of peripheral blood, spleen, and liver.

Tissues were collected and processed for flow cytometric analysis. Peripheral blood was obtained by [FILL: blood collection method and anticoagulant]. Spleens and livers were harvested, mechanically dissociated, and filtered [FILL: filter pore size]. Red blood cells were lysed [FILL: lysis buffer composition and incubation time]. Single-cell suspensions were stained with antibodies targeting human myeloid markers [FILL: complete antibody panel — clone, fluorochrome, vendor, and catalog number for each marker used to identify monocytes, dendritic cells, and granulocytes]. Cells were acquired on [FILL: flow cytometer manufacturer and model] with [FILL: acquisition settings — number of events, flow rate]. Human myeloid populations were identified by sequential gating [FILL: gating strategy details]. Data were analyzed using [FILL: flow cytometry analysis software name and version].

Lipopolysaccharide [FILL: LPS serotype, vendor, and catalog number] was reconstituted [FILL: LPS reconstitution buffer and concentration]. For intraperitoneal challenge, mice received [FILL: exact LPS dose in μg or mg/kg for IP route] by injection into the peritoneal cavity. For intranasal challenge, mice were anesthetized [FILL: anesthetic agent and dose] and received [FILL: exact LPS dose for intranasal route] in [FILL: intranasal administration volume]. Blood and tissues were collected at [FILL: exact time points for sample collection after LPS challenge].

Cytokine concentrations were measured in [FILL: sample type — serum, plasma, or tissue homogenate and processing method]. Samples were analyzed using a Luminex multiplex immunoassay [FILL: Luminex kit manufacturer, catalog number, and complete list of cytokines measured]. The panel quantified NF-κB-dependent cytokines, type I interferon, IL-1β, and IL-18 [FILL: additional cytokines in the panel]. Assays were performed according to the manufacturer's protocol [FILL: any modifications to standard protocol]. Detection limits were [FILL: lower limit of detection for each cytokine].

MCC950 [FILL: vendor and catalog number] was dissolved [FILL: vehicle composition and MCC950 concentration]. Mice received [FILL: exact MCC950 dose in mg/kg] by [FILL: administration route] at [FILL: timing relative to LPS challenge — pretreatment interval or concurrent administration]. Vehicle-treated controls received [FILL: vehicle composition and volume] on the same schedule.

Monocyte viability was assessed by [FILL: cell death assay method — annexin V/PI staining, live/dead dye, or other approach]. Cells were stained with [FILL: viability marker antibodies or dyes — vendor, catalog number, and fluorochrome] and analyzed by flow cytometry. Viable monocytes were identified as [FILL: gating criteria for viable vs. dead cells].

Statistical comparisons were performed using [FILL: specific statistical test — t-test, ANOVA, Mann-Whitney U test, or other]. Each experimental group contained [FILL: number of mice per group (n) for each experiment] mice. Analyses were conducted in [FILL: statistical software name and version]. P values less than 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

research_statement.english: We established that NSG-QUAD mice reconstituted with human hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells develop functional human innate immune cells capable of mounting NF-κB, type I interferon, and NLRP3 inflammasome responses to lipopolysaccharide challenge in vivo.

key_findings: NSG-QUAD mice engrafted with human HSPCs display human monocytes, dendritic cells, and granulocytes in peripheral blood, spleen, and liver by 6 weeks post-injection.
Humanized NSG-QUAD mice respond to intraperitoneal and intranasal LPS with NF-κB-dependent cytokines and type I interferon.
LPS challenge induces NLRP3 inflammasome-mediated IL-1β and IL-18 secretion in humanized NSG-QUAD mice.
MCC950 specifically abrogates IL-1β and IL-18 production without affecting monocyte viability.

novelty_points.point: This is the first demonstration that NSG-QUAD mice support functional human innate immune responses including inflammasome activation in vivo.
novelty_points.evidence_in_input: User states the goal was to 'establish in vivo human inflammatory responses in NSG-QUAD mice' and reports successful LPS-induced cytokine and inflammasome responses.

novelty_points.point: The model enables pharmacological validation of human inflammasome inhibitors in a living system, as shown by MCC950 selectivity.
novelty_points.evidence_in_input: User reports 'MCC950 specifically abrogated IL-1β and IL-18 without affecting monocyte death,' demonstrating target engagement in vivo.

novelty_points.point: Rapid engraftment timeline (6 weeks) makes this model more practical than conventional humanized mouse systems.
novelty_points.evidence_in_input: User states 'NSG-QUAD mice already displayed engraftment... at 6 weeks after HSPC injection.'

well_established: Dysregulated innate immune responses underlie multiple inflammatory diseases.
Preclinical innate immunity research in mice often fails to translate to human clinical outcomes.
NSG-QUAD mice express four human myelopoiesis transgenes to improve human cell engraftment.
LPS activates NF-κB and type I interferon pathways in human myeloid cells.
NLRP3 inflammasome activation leads to IL-1β and IL-18 secretion.
MCC950 is a selective NLRP3 inhibitor.
gap_addressed: Lack of in vivo models that recapitulate human innate immune responses, particularly inflammasome activation.
Uncertainty about whether NSG-QUAD mice support functional human myeloid cell responses beyond engraftment.
Need for preclinical platforms to test human-specific inflammasome inhibitors in a living system.

recommendations.rationale: eLife prioritizes mechanistic immunology studies with strong molecular validation. The demonstration of NF-κB, interferon, and inflammasome pathway functionality in a novel humanized mouse model aligns with eLife's molecular mechanism frame. The pharmacological validation with MCC950 provides the kind of mechanistic depth eLife values. The journal's data-first opening style matches the user's experimental approach.
recommendations.scope_match: eLife regularly publishes humanized mouse models and innate immunity mechanism studies. The combination of model development and functional validation fits the journal's emphasis on tools that enable mechanistic discovery.
recommendations.main_concern: eLife may expect more extensive mechanistic dissection of how NSG-QUAD transgenes enable inflammasome competence, or comparison with other humanized mouse strains.

recommendations.rationale: PNAS publishes high-impact immunology and model organism studies. The study's focus on establishing a translational platform for human innate immunity research fits PNAS scope. The journal's cautious but assertive tone matches the user's balanced presentation of findings.
recommendations.scope_match: PNAS immunology papers often emphasize molecular mechanisms and translational potential. The inflammasome validation and rapid engraftment timeline provide practical utility that PNAS values.
recommendations.main_concern: PNAS may require broader mechanistic insights beyond model validation, such as novel biology revealed by the system or comparison with human patient samples.

recommendations.rationale: PLOS Medicine prioritizes clinical and population health research. While the user emphasizes translational potential, this is fundamentally a model development and mechanistic validation study without direct clinical data.
recommendations.scope_match: The study's focus on improving preclinical-to-clinical translation addresses a gap PLOS Medicine recognizes, but the journal typically requires human cohort data or direct clinical relevance.
recommendations.main_concern: PLOS Medicine's epidemiology/population frame does not align with this mechanistic immunology study. The journal would likely view this as too basic for their scope.

journal_recommendation.rationale_summary: eLife is the strongest fit due to its emphasis on mechanistic immunology and model systems. PNAS is a viable alternative if the authors can frame broader mechanistic or translational implications. PLOS Medicine is not recommended for this study type.

Monocyte viability in MCC950-treated vs. vehicle-treated groups.
NF-κB-dependent cytokines in MCC950-treated vs. vehicle-treated mice, showing no inhibition.

figures_planned.legend_summary: MCC950 specifically blocks IL-1β and IL-18 secretion without reducing monocyte viability or affecting NF-κB-dependent cytokine production, confirming selective NLRP3 inhibition.

clarification_questions.question: What were the quantitative engraftment levels (% human CD45+ cells, absolute counts) for each myeloid subset at 6 weeks?
clarification_questions.reason: Engraftment efficiency determines whether the model is robust enough for routine use and affects interpretation of functional responses.

clarification_questions.question: Were HSPCs from cord blood or bone marrow, and was a single donor or pooled donors used?
clarification_questions.reason: HSPC source affects engraftment kinetics and functional capacity; single vs. pooled donors impacts reproducibility claims.

clarification_questions.question: Which specific NF-κB-dependent cytokines (e.g., TNF-α, IL-6) were detected, and what were their fold-changes?
clarification_questions.reason: Identifying the cytokine profile clarifies which NF-κB-dependent pathways are functional and enables comparison with human in vitro data.

checklist.message: Main text ~2326 words — no official word-count limit for this journal.

article_types.notes: Confirm current list on each curation pass.

required_sections.notes: Research articles use IMRaD; Strengths and Limitations may be required — verify on organization page.

word_limit.notes: PLOS Medicine prefers ≤300 words; maximum 500 words.

structured.notes: Sections: Background; Methods and Findings; Conclusions.

supplementary_policy.value: Supporting information encouraged; not copyedited; publish as submitted.

reporting_checklist.value: Study-type reporting guidelines (CONSORT, STROBE, PRISMA, etc.) as applicable

code_availability.value: Author-generated code should be shared when central to findings

notes.value: In-text citations use numbered brackets [1], [1,2], [1-3] in ascending order of appearance.

raw.disclaimer: Format-readiness checks only. Does not predict editorial acceptance, scope fit, or impact tier.

evidence.sentence: We reconstituted NSG-QUAD mice with human hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells (HSPCs) and examined the development and functional characteristics of human myeloid populations in vivo.  ## Methods and Findings  NSG-QUAD mice received human HSPCs and were assessed for myeloid engraftment at 6 weeks post-injection.

evidence.sentence: This gap impedes preclinical evaluation of inflammasome inhibitors and related innate immune therapeutics in physiologically relevant contexts.  NSG-QUAD mice carry transgenes encoding four human myelopoietic cytokines (M-CSF, IL-3, GM-CSF, and TPO) designed to enhance human myeloid cell differentiation following hematopoietic stem an
evidence.suggestion: Anchor this claim: cite a supporting reference (e.g., [Smith 2020]) or add quantitative evidence (p-value, n, effect size).

evidence.sentence: We further assessed whether pharmacological inflammasome inhibition with MCC950 would selectively block IL-1β and IL-18 release without affecting NF-κB-dependent cytokine expression or monocyte viability, thereby validating this platform for testing human-specific innate immune therapies.
evidence.suggestion: Anchor this claim: cite a supporting reference (e.g., [Smith 2020]) or add quantitative evidence (p-value, n, effect size).

evidence.sentence: represent canonical outputs of NLRP3 inflammasome signaling [CITE: IL-1β and IL-18 as NLRP3 inflammasome products], these findings indicate successful assembly and function of the inflammasome complex within human myeloid cells engrafted in NSG-QUAD mice.  ## MCC950 selectively inhibits inflammasome-mediated cytokines without affecting monocyte viability  To confirm the specificity of inflammasome activation, we administered MCC950, a selective NLRP3 inhibitor.
evidence.suggestion: Anchor this claim: cite a supporting reference (e.g., [Smith 2020]) or add quantitative evidence (p-value, n, effect size).

evidence.sentence: Neither NF-κB-dependent cytokine nor type I interferon concentrations were altered by MCC950 administration (Figure 4D), demonstrating selective inhibition of NLRP3 inflammasome activity without generalized suppression of innate immune responses.
evidence.suggestion: Anchor this claim: cite a supporting reference (e.g., [Smith 2020]) or add quantitative evidence (p-value, n, effect size).

evidence.sentence: urth, whether tissue-resident human macrophages develop in this model is unclear, as our analysis focused on circulating and splenic populations.  Despite these limitations, humanized NSG-QUAD mice provide a platform for testing innate immune therapeutics, including inflammasome inhibitors, within a human cellular context.
evidence.suggestion: Anchor this claim: cite a supporting reference (e.g., [Smith 2020]) or add quantitative evidence (p-value, n, effect size).

evidence.sentence: Potential applications include modeling inflammatory diseases driven by dysregulated NF-κB or inflammasome signaling, particularly as clinical development of inflammasome inhibitors advances [CITE: clinical development of inflammasome inhibitors].  Future studies should characterize additional innate immune pathways—such as cGAS-STING and AIM2 inflammasome responses—to expand the model's utility for studying diverse pattern recognition receptor systems.
evidence.suggestion: Anchor this claim: cite a supporting reference (e.g., [Smith 2020]) or add quantitative evidence (p-value, n, effect size).
